--- SOURCE 1: Cognitive biases in investing: a case in point ---
URL: https://www.norman-k.com/cognitive-biases-in-investing-a-case-in-point/

SUMMARY:
Research in the behavioral finance field has shown that cognitive biases can affect anybody's investment choices.

FULL CONTENT:
![ - Norman K]()

# Cognitive biases in investing: a case in point

## By natural evolution, people are hard-wired to use mental shortcuts when making decisions. *Investing* decisions are no exception to this truth.

## 

![](https://www.norman-k.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/867x578xpexels-photoscom-93398-scaled.jpg.pagespeed.ic.CSrGK_l-O-.jpg)

Research in the behavioral finance field has shown that cognitive biases can affect anybody’s investment choices. In this post, we’ll draw a brief illustration of how these biases enter the investing picture in practice.

Take the case of ***Nikola Corp*** (NKLA), a US-based company operating in the emission-free truck manufacturing sector, for example.

In the days following its listing on Nasdaq in June 2020, Nikola’s public market value ballooned to circa $28bn, a highly unusual mark for an unprofitable early-stage company.

In hindsight, little could justify that kind of valuation from a fundamental standpoint.

Evidently, psychological influences played an outsized role. Three of them come to mind in particular:

### A ride on the passenger seat

First, *Nikola* obviously tries to emulate the success of its peer *Tesla Inc*, starting with its corporate name chosen after late engineer/inventor Nikola Tesla. Consciously or not, many investors were led to draw parallels between the two companies: each recently founded in the US by a seemingly visionary and outspoken CEO/shareholder, each striving to disrupt a segment of the global automotive industry with innovative eco-friendly products. As a result, many must have fallen for the cognitive tendency called the “**Representativeness heuristic**”.

***Representativeness heuristic:*** “when we’re trying to assess how likely a certain event is, we often make our decision by assessing how similar it is to an existing mental prototype”. (1)

![](https://www.norman-k.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/xpexels-le-minh-977213-scaled.jpg.pagespeed.ic.F1yhYLuZbb.jpg)

By the time *Nikola* became publicly-listed, Tesla’s business momentum had already been wildly positive for years, and its market value had increased exponentially to circa $200bn+. With such impressive precedent in mind, Nikola’s valuation upon going public – merely a tenth of Tesla’s after all – seemed a lot more realistic than if Tesla never existed.

### What you see is not what you get

Second, Nikola’s management – and more particularly its former chairman Trevor Milton –  went to extraordinary lengths “selling” the company to investors. Not only did they make countless over-optimistic statements about their business, they also presented information in a way that favors a cognitive bias dubbed the “**framing effect**”.

***Framing effect:*** “when our decisions are influenced by the way information is presented. Equivalent information can be more or less attractive depending on what features are highlighted.”(2)

Consider, for instance, [Nikola’s inaugural quarterly-result press release](https://nikolamotor.com/press_releases/nikola-corporation-reports-second-quarter-results-88), where the company used virtual rendering to imply that its manufacturing site in Germany was nearing completion (knowing that it hadn’t reached mass production a year and a half later).

Or yet more pernicious, when Nikola produced an official video (<https://twitter.com/nikolamotor/status/956576016809340928?lang=fr>) of a truck prototype being driven on a public road, and later was forced into admitting, after a scathing report by short-selling specialist Hindenburg Research, that the vehicle was not powered by an engine but simply rolling down a hill.

### Farewell, fair value

Third, as often the case in high-profile new listings, Nikola’s sky-high valuation found tremendous support in institutional equity research. Investment banks’ median target price at listing was $79 per share (equating to a $28bn valuation), i.e. more than twice the closing price of $33.75 on NKLA’s first trading day. Considering sell-side research is an important reference for many investors, first consensus data implying this upside likely triggered a form of “**Anchoring bias**”.

***Anchoring bias:*** “a cognitive bias that causes us to rely too heavily on the first piece of information we are given about a topic. […] we interpret newer information from the reference point of our anchor, instead of seeing it objectively.”(3)

In a matter of days after its debut, NKLA’s share price soared to $79. But never did it trade at that level again since then. In hindsight, a wildly-unrealistic sell-side consensus which happens to be the peak price of a stock strongly points to anchoring behavior among many buy-side market participants.

Fast forward to today, with NKLA trading around $7 per share, and a far more realistic value of less than $3bn. One quote from investment great Benjamin Graham comes to mind: “*In the short run, the market is a voting machine but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.*”

As Nikola went public in 2020, the “voting” process evidently got caught up in a psychological frenzy. But after the market started “weighing”, things came back to Earth.

(1)(2)(3) : source : https://thedecisionlab.com/biases/

*Nota: the following content is not an investment recommendation to buy or sell the securities mentioned.*

*Norman K Group and its related parties have neither buyer nor seller interests in the securities mentioned.*

### 

# Cognitive biases in trading: Understanding trading psychology with four case studies

![Whatsapp Follow Channel](https://img.etimg.com/photo/104857408.cms)

The Long-Term Capital Management (LTCM) case study involves a hedge fund founded by Nobel laureates and finance experts in 1994 to trade global bond markets. Spectacular success in the initial years, the world's top pedigree and sophisticated mathematical models fed to the overconfidence in 1998 bond arbitrage. The symptoms exhibited for overconfidence were high leverage and ignoring warning signs of the trade going wrong.

# Behavioral Finance Case Studies

Gain diverse insights into Behavioral Finance with structured content exploring theories, biases, strategies, and real-world applications for smarter decisions.

![](https://p16-hera-sg.larksuitecdn.com/tos-alisg-i-hn4qzgxq2n-sg/8055f6941c2640fb974b4475dff17541~tplv-hn4qzgxq2n-png:0:0.png)

Behavioral finance has revolutionized the way we understand financial decision-making by integrating psychology and economics. Unlike traditional finance, which assumes rationality, behavioral finance delves into the cognitive biases, emotions, and social influences that shape our financial behaviors. This article explores the core concepts, challenges, strategies, and real-world applications of behavioral finance through case studies. Whether you're a financial advisor, investor, or business leader, this comprehensive guide will provide actionable insights to help you make smarter financial decisions. By examining proven strategies and leveraging technology, you'll gain a deeper understanding of how to apply behavioral finance principles to achieve better outcomes.

## Understanding the core concepts of behavioral finance case studies

Understanding the core concepts of behavioral finance case studies

### Key Definitions and Principles of Behavioral Finance

Behavioral finance is a field that studies how psychological factors influence financial decision-making. It challenges the traditional assumption of rationality in economics, highlighting how biases, emotions, and heuristics often lead to suboptimal decisions. Key principles include:

Behavioral finance case studies provide a practical lens to understand these principles in action, offering insights into how individuals and organizations can mitigate biases to improve financial outcomes.

### Historical Evolution and Importance of Behavioral Finance

The roots of behavioral finance can be traced back to the 1970s when psychologists like Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky began exploring how cognitive biases affect decision-making. Their groundbreaking work laid the foundation for a new approach to understanding financial behavior. Over the decades, behavioral finance has gained prominence, influencing fields such as investment management, corporate finance, and personal financial planning.

The importance of behavioral finance lies in its ability to bridge the gap between theory and practice. By understanding the psychological underpinnings of financial decisions, professionals can design strategies that account for human behavior, leading to more effective outcomes. Behavioral finance case studies serve as a valuable tool for illustrating these concepts, offering real-world examples of how biases and heuristics impact financial decisions.

## Common challenges and misconceptions about behavioral finance case studies

Common challenges and misconceptions about behavioral finance case studies

### Addressing Myths Surrounding Behavioral Finance

Despite its growing popularity, behavioral finance is often misunderstood. Common myths include:

**Myth 1: Behavioral finance is only for academics.**  
Reality: Behavioral finance has practical applications in investment strategies, financial planning, and corporate decision-making.

**Myth 2: It replaces traditional finance.**  
Reality: Behavioral finance complements traditional finance by addressing its limitations and providing a more holistic understanding of decision-making.

**Myth 3: Biases are always bad.**  
Reality: While biases can lead to errors, they can also serve as adaptive mechanisms in certain contexts.

Behavioral finance case studies help debunk these myths by showcasing its real-world relevance and practical applications.

### Overcoming Barriers to Effective Behavioral Finance Implementation

Implementing behavioral finance principles can be challenging due to several barriers:

To overcome these barriers, organizations can invest in education, leverage technology, and use behavioral finance case studies to demonstrate its value.

## Case studies: success stories using behavioral finance

Case studies: success stories using behavioral finance

### Real-World Examples of Behavioral Finance in Action

Behavioral finance case studies provide valuable insights into how principles are applied in real-world scenarios. Examples include:

**Case Study 1: Overcoming Loss Aversion in Investment Decisions**  
A financial advisor used behavioral nudges to help a client overcome loss aversion, leading to a more diversified and higher-performing portfolio.

**Case Study 2: Reducing Employee Turnover Through Behavioral Insights**  
A company used behavioral finance principles to redesign its employee benefits program, resulting in increased satisfaction and reduced turnover.

**Case Study 3: Improving Retirement Savings with Default Options**  
A retirement plan provider implemented default contribution rates, significantly increasing participation and savings rates.

## Tips for do's and don'ts in behavioral finance

Tips for do's and don'ts in behavioral finance

| **Do's** | **Don'ts** |
| --- | --- |
| Educate yourself and your team on biases. | Ignore the role of emotions in decision-making. |
| Use data-driven insights to guide decisions. | Assume that all clients or stakeholders are rational. |
| Leverage technology to enhance outcomes. | Overcomplicate interventions or strategies. |
| Tailor strategies to individual behaviors. | Apply a one-size-fits-all approach. |
| Continuously evaluate and refine strategies. | Resist adapting to new trends or tools. |

## Faqs about behavioral finance case studies

Faqs about behavioral finance case studies

### What is the primary goal of behavioral finance?

The primary goal of behavioral finance is to understand how psychological factors influence financial decision-making and to develop strategies that account for these influences to improve outcomes.

### How does behavioral finance differ from traditional finance?

Behavioral finance differs from traditional finance by focusing on the psychological and emotional aspects of decision-making, rather than assuming rationality and efficiency in markets.

### What are the most common biases studied in behavioral finance?

Common biases include overconfidence, loss aversion, anchoring, confirmation bias, and herd behavior.

### Can behavioral finance improve investment performance?

Yes, by identifying and mitigating biases, behavioral finance can lead to more informed and effective investment decisions, ultimately improving performance.

### What tools are available for applying behavioral finance insights?

Tools include behavioral analytics platforms, robo-advisors, decision-making frameworks, and behavioral nudges like default options or reminders.

By understanding and applying the principles of behavioral finance through case studies, professionals can make smarter financial decisions, improve outcomes, and drive innovation in the field.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cognitive biases, such as overconfidence and anchoring, heavily influenced decisions in notable finance cases like Long-Term Capital Management, leading to significant market failures.
Cognitive biases like availability and confirmation bias can lead to misdiagnoses in healthcare, as clinicians may overlook critical symptoms due to preconceived notions.
During crises, biases such as groupthink can impair decision-making processes, resulting in ineffective responses, as seen in various organizational failures during emergencies.
Understanding cognitive biases helps professionals develop strategies to mitigate their effects, ultimately improving decision-making outcomes across finance, healthcare, and crisis management.
